summary: Joel shares a quick tip about JPEG file bloat, noting that a small image on his site was 42KB but contained nearly 35KB of useless metadata from a photo editing program. He credits Dennis Forbes for providing a utility to strip the unnecessary data, reducing the image to just 7KB. The post is a brief technical note rather than an essay.
key_points: JPEG files can contain massive metadata blocks ('application blocks') that dwarf the actual image data
In this case, 83% of the file size was useless bloat from photo editing software
Simple tools exist to strip unnecessary JPEG metadata and dramatically reduce file sizes
money_quotes: 34,885 of those bytes were in a useless 'application block' that some photo editing program put there.
key_insight: Photo editing software can silently bloat JPEG files with metadata that vastly exceeds the size of the actual image data.
